english: time, frq.; mi-riṅ-bar (mi ring bar) id. Tar.; dé-nas mi riṅ-bar (de nas mi ring bar) not long after that Tar.; riṅ źig (ring zhig) a long time, riṅ źig lón-pa daṅ (ring zhig lon pa dang) after a long time Dzl.; riṅ źig-tu (ring zhig tu) adv. long, a long while, for a long time, riṅ źig-tu ma _oṅs-pas (ring zhig tu ma 'ongs pas) as he did not come for a long time Dzl.; riṅ źig-na (ring zhig na) after or during a long time Glr.; riṅ-la (ring la), resp. sku-riṅ-la (sku ring la) c. genit. during, at, nyin-gyi riṅ-la (nyin gyi ring la) in the day-time, during the day Pth., „dir bźugs riṅ (dir bzhugs ring) (provinc. for riṅ (ring) ?) _tso-čas ('tsho chas) or rgyags (rgyags) provisions for the time of his stay Mil.; esp. of kings etc.: under a king; during the reign or life of a king, frq.; dé-riṅ (de ring) B., C., *dí-riṅ* (*di ring*) (more correct form, but only in W.) to-day. — 3. old, riṅ źig-na (ring zhig na) Sch. long ago, long since, v. also riṅ-lugs (ring lugs). — II. length, distance etc., more definite form, but of rare occurrence: riṅ-ba-nyíd (ring ba nyid), dé-nas mi-riṅ-ba-nyíd-na (de nas mi ring ba nyid na) a very short time afterwards Tar. Comp. riṅ-kyád (ring khyad) length. — *riṅ-táb* (*ring thab*) W. length, copiousness (of account). — riṅ-túṅ (ring thung) 1. long and short. 2. length, relatively. — riṅ-gág (ring gag), also stod-gág (stod gag) jacket or waistcoat of a Lama, without sleeves.
Cs.: 'the sect or followers of a person', Sch.: 'old customs'; Glr. 92, 2 (?).
('things which are to be preserved for a long time'), धातु, relics of a Buddha or a saint, viz. small, hard particles, acc. to Burnouf the remnants of burnt bones.

swift, speedy, riṅs-par rgyúg-pa (rings par rgyug pa) to run fast, to hasten, hurry; riṅs-par yod (rings par yod) I am in a hurry Mil.; *riṅ-pa toṅ* (*ring pa tong*) W. be quick! make haste! riṅs ruṅ (rings rung) though you be in a hurry Mil.; riṅs-pai bsód-snyoms (rings pa'i bsod snyoms) alms, gifts of charity (requiring haste), urgently requested, and out of the common course, Burn. I, 269. 628 *za-riṅs* (*za rings*), _tuṅ-riṅs* ('thung rings*), waiting impatiently for one's meal, * _grul-riṅs* (* 'grul rings*) for setting out W.; riṅs-stábs-su (rings stabs su) most speedily Mil.
1. meager, emaciated Dzl. and elsewh. — 2. Sch. also: rare.

1. price, value, rin ɣcód-pa (rin gcod pa) to fix, to determine the price Cs. (cf. t'aṅ (thang)), rin rtóg-pa (rin rtog pa) to ascertain the price, to estimate the value Cs.; rin _bébs-pa (rin 'bebs pa) to abate, to lessen the price Cs.; rin _báb-pa (rin 'bab pa), rin _bri-ba (rin 'bri ba) to go down, to sink or fall in value Cs.; *rin tsam* (*rin tsam*) W., *rin gha-tsó* (*rin ga tsho*) C. how dear (is it)? what does it cost? rin-la mi čog (rin la mi chog) Sch. to sell under cost-price; rin-čàn (rin can) dear, costly; rin-méd (rin med) worthless, also: for nothing, gratis; rin-góṅ (rin gong), rin-táṅ (rin thang), rin-tsád (rin tshad) Tar. 202, 17 = rin (rin); rin-čén-po (rin chen po), rin-po-čé (rin po che) v. the next article. — 2. for riṅ (ring), v. riṅ-ba (ring ba) I, 2.

also rin-po-čé (rin po che), 1. very dear, precious, valuable; usually: 2. sbst, रत्न, a precious thing, treasure, jewel, precious stone, precious metal; metal in general; Glr. 7, five jewels of the gods are enumerated, sapphire, indragopa and other three, prob. fabulous, stones, and five jewels as the property of man: gold, silver, pearls, corals, lapis lazuli; in other books other jewels are specified as such. In the Buddha-legends frq. mention is made of the rin-po-čé sna bdun (rin po che sna bdun), i.e. the extraordinary treasures of a Tshakravartin king, viz. the precious wheel (v. _kór-lo ('khor lo)), the precious elephant, the precious horse, the precious jewel, the precious wife, the precious minister and the precious general (or inst. of him, the precious citizen) v. Gyatch. chap. III. Sometimes rin-po-čéi (rin po che'i) may be understood literally: consisting of jewels, of precious stones, at other times it is merely equivalent to: valuable, precious; rin-po-čéi gliṅ (rin po che'i gling) Glr. seems frq. to signify a holy, happy land inhabited by gods. — 3. a title, used not only in rgya-mtso rin-po-čé (rgya mtsho rin po che) and paṇ-čén rin-po-čé (pan chen rin po che) (the honorary titles of the high-priests of Lhasa and of Tashilunpo), but also a title of every Lama of a higher class.
= ri-ba (ri ba) Sch. (Dzl. IV, 8. 15, and in Sch.'s dictionary): worth, costing,
